evals.name: interview-guide-b2b-saas
evals.prompt: I'm building an AI tool that automates invoice processing for accounts payable teams at mid-market companies (100-500 employees). I need an interview guide for problem discovery — I want to understand how AP teams process invoices today and where the biggest pain points are. Target interviewee is the AP Manager.
evals.expected_output: A structured interview guide with protocol notes, question banks organized by phase (opening, current process, pain points, workarounds, prioritization, closing), and interview style rules — ready to use in a 45-minute discovery interview.
expectations: The interview guide includes protocol notes that remind the interviewer to listen 80% and talk 20% — this is the most violated rule in customer discovery and the guide must reinforce it

Questions are organized by phase (opening, current process, pain points, workarounds, prioritization, closing) with time allocations — unstructured interviews produce scattered data that cannot be synthesized across interviewees
No question mentions the AI tool or solution concept — problem discovery must be separated from solution validation; mentioning the product leads to agreement bias that produces false positives
Questions use open-ended formats ('walk me through...', 'tell me about...') rather than closed yes/no formats — closed questions produce agreement bias, not insight
The guide includes probing follow-ups ('tell me more about that', 'what do you do when that happens?') — probing is what turns surface-level answers into deep insight
The closing section includes a referral request — the most valuable output of an interview is access to the next interviewee, and failing to ask is the most common discovery mistake

evals.name: interview-synthesis-5-interviews
evals.prompt:
I've completed 5 discovery interviews with AP managers at mid-market companies. Here are my raw notes:

Interview 1 (Sarah, AP Manager, 200-person manufacturing): Spends 3 days/month on invoice matching. Main pain is purchase order mismatches. Uses Excel to track. Said 'I basically live in Excel during close week.'

Interview 2 (James, Controller, 150-person services): AP team of 2 handles 400 invoices/month. Duplicate invoices are biggest issue — caught $45K duplicate last quarter. No automated matching.

Interview 3 (Maria, AP Lead, 300-person retail): Three-way matching takes 15 min per invoice. ERP doesn't handle exceptions. 'Every exception is a phone call to purchasing.'

Interview 4 (David, AP Manager, 180-person tech): Vendor follow-up consumes 40% of team time. Late payments cause vendor relationship issues. 'We lose early payment discounts every month.'

Interview 5 (Priya, Finance Director, 250-person healthcare): Compliance documentation for each invoice adds 10 min. Audit prep takes 2 weeks. 'I dread audit season — we're always scrambling for documentation.'

Synthesize these interviews.
evals.expected_output: A structured synthesis with JTBD map, pain ranking matrix, at least 3 non-obvious insight statements, vivid customer quotes, and identification of what still needs to be learned.
expectations: The JTBD map includes functional, related, emotional, and social jobs — limiting to functional jobs misses the emotional and social dimensions that often drive purchasing decisions
The pain ranking uses frequency x severity scoring across all 5 interviews — ranking by frequency alone misses high-severity pains mentioned by fewer interviewees
At least 3 insight statements are produced and each is non-obvious (not 'AP teams want faster processing') — weak insights lead to obvious solutions that don't differentiate
Vivid customer quotes are included with role and company type attribution — direct quotes make the pain real for stakeholders who weren't in the room
A 'What We Still Need to Learn' section identifies specific unknowns — 5 interviews is the minimum for pattern detection and the synthesis must flag where confidence is low
Pain ranking surfaces invoice matching/PO mismatches as a high-frequency pain (mentioned by 3+ interviewees in different forms) — this cross-interview pattern is the primary signal

evals.name: hmw-problem-statement
evals.prompt: Based on our discovery with AP teams, the top pain is three-way invoice matching which takes 15+ minutes per invoice and requires manual exception handling via phone calls. 4 of 5 interviewees described some version of this pain. Generate HMW problem statements.
evals.expected_output: 5 HMW variants approaching the problem through different lenses (solution, outcome, emotional, constraint, systemic), with a recommended primary and rationale grounded in the discovery data.
expectations: Exactly 5 HMW variants are produced, each through a different lens (solution, outcome, emotional, constraint, systemic) — fewer than 5 narrows the solution space prematurely

Each HMW is specific enough to focus ideation but open enough not to prescribe the solution — 'How might we automate invoice matching?' is too prescriptive; 'How might we reduce exception resolution time?' is better
Each HMW references the discovery data (e.g., '15+ minutes per invoice', 'phone calls to purchasing') — HMW statements disconnected from evidence lead to solutions that don't address real pain
A recommended primary HMW is stated with rationale explaining why it is the best starting point for ideation — without a recommendation, the founder faces choice paralysis
The constraint lens HMW works with the existing workaround rather than replacing it — this lens often produces the most adoptable solutions because it respects current behavior

evals.name: negative-idea-generation
evals.prompt: I have a problem statement about invoice processing pain. Can you brainstorm 50 product ideas that could solve this? I want a full idea sprint.
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the idea skill, since idea generation and brainstorming are outside the discovery skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is an idea generation request and redirects to the idea skill — discovery is for understanding customers and problems, not generating solution ideas
The agent does NOT produce a list of product ideas within the discovery skill — doing so would bypass the idea skill's DVF scoring framework and structured 100-idea sprint format

evals.name: negative-pilot-analysis
evals.prompt: We ran a 4-week pilot with 3 AP teams. Here are the results: 71% adoption rate, 40% reduction in matching time, NPS of 68. Can you analyze whether we should continue or pivot?
evals.expected_output: The agent should redirect this to the validate skill, since pilot results analysis and pivot decisions are outside the discovery skill's scope.
expectations: The agent recognizes this is a pilot results analysis request and redirects to the validate skill — discovery is for pre-product customer research, not post-pilot analysis
The agent does NOT produce a BML analysis or pivot recommendation within the discovery skill — doing so would bypass the validate skill's evidence quality standards and pivot decision framework
